lead: Booking a removals team can feel like one of those jobs you keep meaning to get to and never quite do. Yet of all the things on a moving checklist, it is the one most worth tackling early. Since 2005 our family-run firm in Westerfield, just outside Ipswich, has guided thousands of Suffolk households through the process, and we have found that a well-booked move is already half a calm one. Here is exactly how to go about it, from first enquiry to confirmed date.

sections.h: Gather the Details Before You Pick Up the Phone
sections.body:
Before you contact anyone, it helps to have a rough picture of your move clear in your own head. You do not need precise measurements or a full inventory; a sensible idea of how many bedrooms you are leaving, roughly how full the house is, and where you are heading is enough to get a useful conversation going. The more honestly you can describe the job, the more accurate the answer you will receive in return.

Think too about the awkward extras that are easy to forget. The loft full of boxes, the chest freezer in the garage, the piano in the front room, the shed that needs emptying. In our experience these are exactly the things that get left out of an early phone call and then cause a scramble later. Jotting them down before you ring saves everyone time and gives us a true sense of the work involved.

It is also worth knowing your likely dates, even if they are not yet set in stone. Moves cluster around the end of the month and over the summer, so the sooner you can flag a rough window, the easier it is for us to hold something suitable. A date that might still wobble is far more useful to share than no date at all.

sections.h: Ask for a Proper Survey, Not Just a Quote
sections.body:
Anyone can give you a number over the phone, but a number plucked from a brief description is rarely the number you end up paying. That is why we always offer to come and look at the job properly, either in person or over a video call if that suits you better. A survey lets us see the volume with our own eyes, judge access at both ends, and spot anything that might slow the day down before it becomes a surprise.

During that survey, walk us through everything. Show us the narrow staircase, the heavy oak wardrobe that will not fit through the door in one piece, the gravel drive the lorry cannot quite reach. None of this puts us off; our crews deal with it every week across Suffolk. What it does is let us plan the right vehicle, the right number of people and the right kit, so the quote we give you genuinely reflects the day ahead.

A good survey is also your chance to ask questions. How long will the move take? Will the same crew handle both ends? What happens if your completion date slips? We would far rather answer all of that calmly in your living room than leave you guessing. By the time the survey is done, you should feel you understand exactly what you are booking.

sections.h: Decide How Much You Want Us to Do
sections.body:
One of the biggest factors in your booking is simply how much of the work you want to keep for yourself. Some customers enjoy packing and only want us to carry and drive. Others would happily never see a roll of tape and ask us to pack the entire house. Plenty land somewhere in the middle, packing the everyday clutter themselves and leaving the fragile china and the wardrobes to us.

There is no right answer here, only the answer that fits your time, your budget and your nerves. We have found that customers who are short on time but not on money tend to hand over the packing gladly, while those happy to spend a few evenings wrapping glasses prefer to save a little. The important thing is to decide before you book, so the quote reflects the real scope and nobody is surprised on the day.

If you do choose a full packing service, we will usually come the day before to wrap and box everything, then return on moving day itself to load and transport. Settling this early also lets us bring the correct amount of materials, so we are never short of boxes halfway through your kitchen.

sections.h: Confirm the Date and Get It in Writing
sections.body:
Once you are happy with the survey and the quote, the next step is to pin down a date. We always recommend booking as soon as you are reasonably confident, even if your completion is not fully fixed, because the good slots disappear quickly in busy periods. A provisional booking that you adjust later is far safer than waiting until everything is certain and finding nothing is left.

When you confirm, make sure you receive the agreement in writing, with the date, the address at both ends, the scope of work and the price all set out clearly. This is not about distrust; it simply means everyone is working from the same understanding. In our experience the moves that run smoothest are the ones where nothing important was ever left to memory or a hurried phone call.

Keep that paperwork somewhere easy to find, and read it through once it arrives. If anything looks wrong, the time to say so is now, not on moving morning. A quick check today saves a difficult conversation later, and it gives you the quiet confidence that the most important part of your move is genuinely sorted.

sections.h: Stay in Touch as the Day Approaches
sections.body:
Booking your move is not quite the end of the conversation. House moves rarely run in a perfectly straight line, and dates shift more often than anyone would like, particularly when a chain is involved. The best thing you can do is keep us informed. If your completion date moves, your solicitor goes quiet, or your plans change, a quick call lets us adjust rather than being caught out at the last moment.

We would always rather hear from you early, even with bad news, than discover a problem on the morning itself. Because we are a family-run firm and not a faceless call centre, you will usually speak to the same people throughout, which makes those updates far easier. A short message a week before to reconfirm the details keeps everything fresh and leaves no room for crossed wires.

As the day nears, we will also confirm practical points such as parking for the lorry, access at both properties and roughly what time we expect to arrive. The more we both know in advance, the less there is to think about when the morning finally comes. Keep the lines open and your booking turns into a smooth, predictable moving day rather than an anxious wait.

Once your booking is confirmed, the pressure genuinely lifts, because the single hardest decision is behind you. From here it becomes a matter of working through the smaller jobs in good time: redirecting your post, sorting the utilities, packing room by room and labelling clearly. None of these are difficult on their own, and with a removals team already secured you can tackle them at a relaxed pace rather than in a panic.

On our side, the work continues quietly in the background. We schedule the right crew and vehicle, prepare the materials you have asked for, and make sure everyone knows the details of your particular job. Because we have been moving Suffolk families since 2005, much of this runs like clockwork, and you rarely need to think about it at all until the day arrives.

When moving day comes, a well-handled booking shows. The lorry turns up when expected, the crew already know the score, and the awkward items have been planned for rather than discovered. That is the whole point of booking carefully and early: not to make the move complicated, but to make the day itself feel almost uneventful. In our experience, an uneventful moving day is the very best kind there is.

faqs: How far in advance should I book my removals?

As soon as you have a date you are reasonably confident in. Good slots fill quickly around month-end and over the summer, so an early provisional booking that you adjust later is far safer than waiting until everything is certain and finding nothing suitable is left.
Do I need a survey before booking?

It genuinely helps. A proper survey, in person or by video call, lets us judge the volume, check access at both ends and spot anything tricky, so the quote reflects the real job. A number given over the phone from a brief description is rarely the number you end up paying.
Can I change my moving date after booking?

Yes, and you would not be the first. Chains slip and completion dates wobble all the time. Just let us know as early as you can and we will do our best to adjust. As a family-run firm you will usually speak to the same people throughout, which makes those changes far easier.
What information do you need from me to book?

A rough idea of the size of your home, how full it is, where you are moving to, your likely dates, and any awkward items such as a piano, a chest freezer or a loft full of boxes. The more honestly you describe it, the more accurate the quote and the plan will be.
